articles.headline: Why are there hydration breaks at the 2026 World Cup, and why has FIFA been criticised?
articles.description: All 104 matches at the 2026 World Cup are stopping for two hydration breaks. Why have they been stipulated by FIFA, and why have they been criticised?

images.name:
Players of Tunisia take drinks at the hydration break during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group F match between Sweden and Tunisia at Monterrey Stadium
 [1296x729]
images.alt: Players of Tunisia take drinks at the hydration break during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group F match between Sweden and Tunisia at Monterrey Stadium

images.name: Enzo Fernandez #8 of Chelsea FC drinks water in a hydration break during the FIFA Club World Cup 2025 semi-final match between Fluminense FC and Chelsea FC at MetLife Stadium [1296x729]
images.caption: Argentina's Enzo Fernández said he felt "dizzy" while playing in "very dangerous" temperatures during last summer's Club World Cup in the United States.
images.alt: Enzo Fernandez #8 of Chelsea FC drinks water in a hydration break during the FIFA Club World Cup 2025 semi-final match between Fluminense FC and Chelsea FC at MetLife Stadium

images.name:
Electronic screen displaying hydration break information during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group C match between Haiti and Scotland at Boston Stadium
 [1296x729]
images.caption: Advertising is prominent on stadium screens during drinks breaks at the World Cup.
images.alt: Electronic screen displaying hydration break information during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group C match between Haiti and Scotland at Boston Stadium

images.name:
Virgil van Dijk (Netherlands) looks on during Group F FIFA World Cup 2026, Netherlands and Japan, Dallas Stadium
 [1296x729]
images.caption: Virgil van Dijk has been critical of the hydration breaks introduced at this summer's World Cup.
images.alt: Virgil van Dijk (Netherlands) looks on during Group F FIFA World Cup 2026, Netherlands and Japan, Dallas Stadium

images.name:
The Brazil team talk during a hydration break during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group C match between Brazil and Morocco at New York New Jersey Stadium
 [1296x729]
images.caption: Carlo Ancelotti was thankful for the opportunity to speak to his players when Brazil found themselves a goal behind to Morocco in the first half of their World Cup opener.
images.alt: The Brazil team talk during a hydration break during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group C match between Brazil and Morocco at New York New Jersey Stadium

images.name:
Myung-Bo Hong, Head Coach of Korea Republic, gives his players instructions during a hydration break during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group A match between Korea Republic and Czechia
 [1296x729]
images.caption: Hydration breaks provide a window for coaches to gather their players and tweak their tactics.
images.alt: Myung-Bo Hong, Head Coach of Korea Republic, gives his players instructions during a hydration break during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group A match between Korea Republic and Czechia

images.name:
Germany's forward #26 Deniz Undav, Germany's midfielder #13 Pascal Gross, Germany's midfielder #17 Florian Wirtz, Germany's midfielder #05 Aleksandar Pavlovic and teammates celebrate
 [1296x729]
images.caption: Drinks breaks could have a major effect on the outcome of matches at the World Cup.
images.alt: Germany's forward #26 Deniz Undav, Germany's midfielder #13 Pascal Gross, Germany's midfielder #17 Florian Wirtz, Germany's midfielder #05 Aleksandar Pavlovic and teammates celebrate
images.credit: Kirill KUDRYAVTSEV / AFP via Getty Images

article.headline: World Cup 2022: Ecuador beat Qatar in tournament opener
article.description: Enner Valencia's two first-half goals led Ecuador to a dominant 2-0 victory over Qatar on Sunday.
article.linkText: Ecuador beat hosts Qatar as World Cup begins

images.name: Why did Ecuador have a goal disallowed vs. Qatar?
images.caption: Dale Johnson explains why Ecuador had a goal ruled out in confusing circumstances in the World Cup opener vs. Qatar.

video.headline: Why did Ecuador have a goal disallowed vs. Qatar?
video.caption: Dale Johnson explains why Ecuador had a goal ruled out in confusing circumstances in the World Cup opener vs. Qatar.
video.title: Why did Ecuador have a goal disallowed vs. Qatar?
video.description: Dale Johnson explains why Ecuador had a goal ruled out in confusing circumstances in the World Cup opener vs. Qatar.

tracking.trackingName: COM_SOC Analysis (FWC) (Why did Ecuador have a goal disallowed vs. Qatar?) 20221120 GLOBAL

article.story:
Ecuador eased past World Cup debutants Qatar 2-0 on Sunday with two goals from veteran striker Enner Valencia in the first opening-game defeat for a host nation in the tournament's history.

Valencia scored an early penalty and added a well-taken 31st-minute header to give the South Americans a commanding first-half lead as the Qataris looked overcome by the significance of the occasion.

- Stream on ESPN+: LaLiga, Bundesliga, more (U.S.)

"Obviously this was not what we were hoping for," Qatar coach Felix Sanchez said.

"There is no excuse, I want to congratulate our opponents Ecuador. There is a lot of room for improvement.

"We did not play at our best level. Maybe the responsibility and the nerves got the better of us. We just have to let go and forget about it and be more competitive. We can do it."

It could have been even worse for the hosts in front of a 67,372-fan crowd at the Al Bayt Stadium had Valencia, 33, not had a third-minute header disallowed for a marginal offside after goalkeeper Saad al-Sheeb had flapped at the ball.

It was al-Sheeb who later tripped Valencia when he was through on goal and then dived the wrong way as the Ecuadorian calmly dispatched the resulting penalty in the 16th minute.

- Borden: Opener makes clear this will be WC like no other
- World Cup roundup: Benzema out, beer sales nixed, Infantino rants

For his second, Valencia was unmarked but still showed great technique to stoop and take Angelo Preciado's cross almost from behind him to head past al-Sheeb again.

"I dreamt so many times about winning this opening match. This was a dream that I managed to score and help my team get the three points," Valencia said.

"We managed to score a couple of quick goals which helped us to get control of the game and win the three points. ... We think we can go very far and perform well."

That goal killed the game as a contest, and the second half was played at a slow pace, with many Qatar fans leaving before the end as Ecuador's fans chanted "Olé!"

Despite an enviously long time to bond and train together, including summer camps in Europe, Qatar looked largely toothless. Almoez Ali had their best chance at the end of the first half, heading wide but then judged offside.

Mohammed Muntari also fired over in the dying minutes.

Both sides now have to play Senegal and the Netherlands in the group stage, with Qatar's chances of picking up points or qualifying looking slim on this performance.

Ecuador are dreaming of surpassing their best previous World Cup showing, when they reached the last 16 in 2006.
